lesson.title: Factoring by Grouping
lesson.body: **Factoring by grouping** works on 4-term polynomials. Steps: (1) Group the first two and last two terms. (2) Factor the GCF out of each group. (3) If both groups share a common binomial factor, factor it out. Example: x³ + 2x² + 3x + 6 = x²(x + 2) + 3(x + 2) = (x² + 3)(x + 2). The key signal: after factoring each pair, you should see the same binomial in parentheses.

rooms.question: Factor by grouping: x³ + 3x² + 2x + 6
rooms.explanation: Group: (x³+3x²)+(2x+6) = x²(x+3)+2(x+3). Factor out (x+3): (x²+2)(x+3). Check by expanding: x³+3x²+2x+6 ✓.

rooms.question: Factor: 2x³ − x² + 6x − 3
rooms.explanation: Group: (2x³−x²)+(6x−3) = x²(2x−1)+3(2x−1). Factor out (2x−1): (x²+3)(2x−1). Both groups must reveal the same binomial — that's the signal grouping worked.

rooms.question: Factor: x³ − 2x² − 9x + 18
rooms.explanation: Group: x²(x−2)−9(x−2) = (x²−9)(x−2). Then factor x²−9 as a difference of squares: (x−3)(x+3). Final: (x−3)(x+3)(x−2). Always factor completely — (x²−9) can be factored further.

rooms.question: Order the grouping steps to factor: 5x³ + 10x² − 3x − 6
rooms.explanation: Group → factor GCF from each group → identify common binomial → factor it out → verify. Note: −3(x+2) means −3·x=−3x and −3·2=−6, preserving the negative signs from the original.

boss.name: The Grouping Wraith
boss.taunt: Two by two into the abyss.

questions.question: When trying to factor 2x³ + 3x² + 4x + 6, which grouping produces a common binomial?
questions.explanation: Grouping 1: (2x³+4x)+(3x²+6) = 2x(x²+2)+3(x²+2) = (2x+3)(x²+2) ✓. Grouping 2: (2x³+3x²)+(4x+6) = x²(2x+3)+2(2x+3) = (x²+2)(2x+3) ✓. Both give the same result — the order inside doesn't change the product.

questions.question: Factor completely: 6x³ − 9x² + 4x − 6
questions.explanation: Group: (6x³−9x²)+(4x−6) = 3x²(2x−3)+2(2x−3) = (3x²+2)(2x−3). Note: options B and C are the same expression — (2x−3)(3x²+2) = (3x²+2)(2x−3). Both B and C are correct; the expected canonical form is C.
